metadata.title: Hierarchy in Regime Complexes: Understanding Authority in Antarctic Governance
authors: F G R E E N

abstract:
Many scholars argue that regime complexes are nonhierarchical. However, if that is true, then how does authority function? This article argues that the conceptualization of regime complexes as largely devoid of hierarchy is mistaken. Instead, it offers a new definition of regime complexes: emergent patterns of authority among state and non-state actors, which vary in their degree of hierarchy. Hierarchy in regime complexes looks different from political scientists' traditional conceptualization. It is systemic, emergent, and positional. I present two dimensions of variation in hierarchy: deference and autonomy. These dimensions provide both a conceptual and an empirical strategy for understanding how authority relations are constituted. Conceptually, they allow us to "see" hierarchy in regime complexes. Empirically, they provide transparent, replicable and variable measures, which have eluded much of the work to date. I use topic modeling coupled with network analysis to detect hierarchy in the regime complex for Antarctica. I demonstrate that the inclusion of non-state actors and their governance activities changes our understanding of the Antarctic regime complex. This approach reveals a hierarchical regime complex, where some non-state actors have considerable authority and are governing issues not regulated by formal rules.

Muchos académicos sostienen que los complejos de régimen no son jerárquicos. Pero si eso es cierto, ¿cómo funciona la autoridad? Este documento sostiene que la conceptualización de los complejos de régimen como algo desprovisto de jerarquía es errónea. En cambio, ofrece una nueva definición de los complejos de régimen: patrones emergentes de autoridad entre actores del estado y ajenos a él que varían en su grado de jerarquía. La jerarquía en los complejos de regímenes es diferente a la conceptualización tradicional de los politólogos. Es sistémica, emergente y posicional. Presento dos dimensiones de variación de la jerarquía: la sumisión y la autonomía. Estas dimensiones ofrecen una estrategia tanto conceptual como empírica para entender cómo se constituyen las relaciones de autoridad. Desde el punto de vista conceptual, nos permiten "ver" la jerarquía en los complejos de régimen. Desde el punto de vista empírico, proporcionan medidas transparentes, replicables y variables, que no se han tenido en cuenta en gran parte de los trabajos realizados hasta la fecha. Utilizo la modelización de temas junto con el análisis de redes para detectar la jerarquía en el complejo de regímenes de la Antártida. Demuestro que la inclusión de actores al margen del estado y sus actividades de gobernanza cambia nuestra comprensión del complejo del régimen antártico. Este enfoque revela un complejo de regímenes jerárquicos, en el que algunos actores al margen del estado tienen una autoridad considerable y rigen cuestiones no reguladas por normas oficiales. Nombre de chercheurs soutiennent que les complexes de régime ne sont pas hiérarchiques. Mais si tel est le cas, comment l'autorité fonctionne-t-elle? Cet article affirme que la conceptualisation des complexes de régime comme étant en grande partie dépourvus de hiérarchie est une méprise. Au lieu de cela, il propose une nouvelle définition des complexes de régime: modèles émergents d'autorité entre acteurs étatiques et non étatiques qui varient dans leur degré de hiérarchie. La hiérarchie des complexes de régime semble différente de celle qui est exposée dans la conceptualisation traditionnelle des politologues. Elle est systémique, émergente et positionnelle. Je présente deux dimensions de variation de hiérarchie: la déférence et l'autonomie. Ces dimensions offrent à la fois une stratégie conceptuelle et une stratégie empirique pour comprendre la manière dont les relations d'autorité sont constituées. D'un point de vue conceptuel, elles nous permettent de « percevoir » la hiérarchie des complexes de régime. Et d'un point de vue empirique, elles offrent des mesures transparentes, reproductibles et variables qui ont jusqu'ici échappé à une grande partie des travaux menés. J'ai utilisé une modélisation thématique associée à une analyse de réseau pour déceler la hiérarchie du complexe de régime de l'Antarctique. Je démontre que l'inclusion des acteurs non étatiques et de leurs activités de gouvernance change notre compréhension du complexe de régime de l'Antarctique. Cette approche révèle un complexe de régime hiérarchique dans lequel certains acteurs non étatiques ont un pouvoir considérable et régissent des questions qui ne sont pas réglementées par des règles formelles.

sections.paper_id: Green - 2022 - Hierarchy in Regime Complexes Understanding Authority in Antarctic Governance
paragraphs: The governance of Antarctica is considered an unequivocal success of global environmental cooperation. In a series of multilateral agreements, states commit to forego sovereignty claims, protect the environment, and cooperate in scientific research. Over time, new actors-including non-governmental organizations (NGOs), scientific organizations and business actors-have emerged to participate in its governance. Yet, the rise of commercial activities, such Jessica F. Green is Associate Professor of Political Science at the University of Toronto, Canada. Her research examines the political economy of climate change, with a particular focus on the role of non-state actors. She is the author of Rethinking Private Authority, published by the Princeton University Press in 2014.
as tourism and bioprospecting, in Antarctica is creating new challenges.

In this article, I examine variation in authority across the diversity of actors involved in Antarctic governance to understand whether and how they can meet these new challenges. The rise of non-state and transnational actors and the proliferation of new institutional forms have produced "regime complexity," defined as "a collective of partially overlapping and nonhierarchical regimes" (Raustiala and Victor 2004, 279). According to this commonly used definition, and many subsequent interpretations, regime complexes (RCs) are largely devoid of hierarchy. Instead, rules and organizations increasingly bump into each other, since there is no immediate way to adjudicate among conflicts.

This article challenges the received wisdom on regime complexity in two ways. First, in concert with recent works, it argues for an explicit focus on hierarchy (Henning 2017;Pratt 2018;Henning and Pratt 2020). While some scholarship addresses related concepts, such as the degree of fragmentation or integration in RCs, very few refer explicitly to hierarchy (Biermann et al. 2009;Gehring and Faude 2013;Gómez-Mera 2015). Others acknowledge variation in how conflicts are resolved, but still begin from the basic assumption that RCs are nonhierarchical (Alter and Meunier 2009;Drezner 2009;Keohane and Victor 2011;Keohane, Colgan, and Van de Graaf 2011;Orsini, Morin, and Young 2013). Bringing hierarchy to the fore is important for understanding the political implications of regime complexity: it reveals who has authority.

Second, this study expands the potential universe of hierarchical relations through an examination of governance activities, rather than a narrower focus on formal rules; it therefore expands the potential for discovering hierarchical authority relations. The regime complexity literature generally focuses on formal legal relations among states and international organizations (IOs). In general, it has overestimated the importance of rules and underestimated the need to study governance activities. A focus on rules limits the potential for hierarchy, since there are few formal mechanisms to adjudicate among them. In this study, hierarchy is constructed in an informal manner, as opposed to a strictly legal one.

I explicitly reject the standard definition of RCs as nonhierarchical since it precludes an ordering principle of governance arrangements. Specifically, RCs can be understood as: emergent patterns of authority among state and non-state actors, which vary in their degree of hierarchy. 1 This definition indicates that hierarchical relations among actors in world politics can emerge without an orderer. Moreover, hierarchy looks different from standard contractual conceptions in which subordinate actors trade some degree of freedom for the provision of order by dominant actors (Lake 2009). Hierarchy is systemic, emergent, and positional. (I refer to this as the SEP framework.) I suggest two dimensions of variation-the extent to which actors defer to each other and the variation in autonomy among actors-and offer a strategy for measurement.

This article makes four key contributions to the studies of regime complexity and, more broadly, our understanding of the functioning of authority in global governance. First, it provides a revised definition, which clearly highlights variation in hierarchy. Second, this study provides transparent, replicable, and variable measures that have eluded much of the work to date (for recent exceptions, see Henning 2017;Pratt 2018;Henning and Pratt 2020). Many scholars argue that an increasing number of issue areas are characterized by RCs. However, if all governance is complex, then regime complexity is simply a background condition of world politics, since there is no variation. Instead, I suggest ways to measure variation through the lens of hierarchy.

Third, whereas most studies of regime complexity focus on exclusively formal international institutions (for exceptions, see Abbott 2012;Green and Auld 2017), this study includes non-state actors as sources of authority. An exclusive focus on the formal rules would necessarily exclude their authority; the analysis presented here shows how this can skew our understanding of a given RC.
1 Non-state actors may include (but are not limited to): civil society and subnational, transnational, and private actors.

Fourth, to measure hierarchy, I use topic modeling coupled with network analysis to study variation in authority in the RC governing Antarctica-the intergovernmental and transnational organizations involved in the management and protection of Antarctic lands and species. I use topic modeling to identify the issues governed and then employ social network analysis (SNA) to discover which organizations are connected through work on shared topics.

Three key findings emerge. First, there is evidence of a regulatory gap in Antarctic governance: bioprospectingthe search for genetic materials that can be used for commercial or medical purposes-is not being addressed. Second, the governance of tourism occurs largely through private tour operators rather than through multilateral cooperation. Thus, there is a group of non-state actors with considerable authority, which would not be evident through traditional analyses of regime complexity. Third, Antarctic governance is quite hierarchical. There is a large division of labor among organizations, and three organizations are particularly authoritative-spanning the scientific, NGO, and multilateral sectors. This bodes well for future cooperation; should they choose to address emerging issues, these three organizations are well-positioned to do so.

These findings have broader implications for understanding the structure and function of global governance. They illustrate that hierarchy may emerge through the act of governing. Thus, we must distinguish between hierarchy and hierarchization (Kim 2020). Hierarchization occurs when states create authority relations by design. However, hierarchy can occur without the explicit direction of states. This distinction implies that scholars may have underestimated which actors are authoritative in a given issue area and the degree of authority they possess.

Moreover, hierarchy in RCs means that strategic actions may be less available or necessary than what the previous work suggests. Much of the work on RCs examines the consequences of conflict and the ability to exit. As a result, states and IOs engage in behavior such as forum shopping, forum shifting, or leveraging strategic inconsistency (Alter and Meunier 2009;Betts 2009;Helfer 2009). However, if a hierarchy among organizations emerges through governing, these strategic actions may become less necessary. 2Finally, as a corollary to the previous point, emergent hierarchy could potentially provide pathways to cooperation that were previously unobserved. Hale and Held document how diverse organizations can coalesce around common goals (Hale and Held 2017). Including non-state actors in the institutional mix can create conditions for emergent hierarchy to generate cooperation. This can also work in less desirable ways, empowering actors "behind the scenes" with little transparency or accountability (Kalyanpur and Newman 2021).

The article proceeds as follows. First, I discuss the weaknesses of the RC literature. Second, I describe how we can "see" hierarchy in RCs through the SEP framework and offer two observable implications: deference and autonomy. Third, I provide an overview of Antarctic governance. Fourth, I describe the use of topic modeling to understand which issues are being governed in Antarctica. I then combine this with SNA to see how actors are connected through their work on shared topics. Together, these two methods show how patterns of hierarchy emerge, providing a picture of the authority landscape in Antarctic governance. The final section suggests how this approach might be applied more broadly to the study of world politics.

sections.paper_id: Green - 2022 - Hierarchy in Regime Complexes Understanding Authority in Antarctic Governance
paragraphs: The study of complexity in international relations (IR) literature is not new. 3 Early works describe various aspects of complexity using different labels. Jervis recognizes that world politics is rife with "system effects," which can be easily understood as the ways in which complex systems behave (Jervis 1998). Aggarwal focuses on how new institutions are "reconciled" with the existing ones. In some cases, nesting occurs, and institutions are reshuffled hierarchically. In others, there is a division of labor to eliminate conflict (Aggarwal 1998).

More recently, Raustiala and Victor coined the term "regime complex" to describe the multilateral agreements that govern the management of plant genetic resources. This concept has since been applied to multiple issue areas (see, e.g., Betts 2009;Keohane and Victor 2011;Struett, Nance, and Armstrong 2013). Abbott (2012) subsequently expanded upon their work in describing a "transnational regime complex," which includes non-state and subnational actors. While these studies have advanced our collective understanding of how the topology of global governance is changing, they suffer from four shortcomings. First and most importantly, the emphasis on nonhierarchical institutions leaves few conceptual tools to understand how authority relations are organized. Second, the focus on rules biases toward an absence of hierarchy, since there is no single body with the authority to adjudicate among them. Third, the comparison of "like with like" often excludes the diversity of actors and governance arrangements. Finally, there is a lack of replicable measures. The SEP framework offers a way to overcome these shortcomings.

paragraphs: First, many studies of regime complexity equate the proliferation of rules with absence of hierarchy (Drezner 2009;Hafner-Burton 2009;Gómez-Mera 2015;Alter and Raustiala 2018). Orsini and colleagues note that interactions among regimes in a regime complex are "potentially problematic" because of "intransitive" relations among elemental regimes; that is, there is no hierarchy to resolve conflicts among rules (Orsini, Morin, and Young 2013). Alter and Meunier state that "although rule complexity also exists in the domestic realm, the lack of hierarchy distinguishes international regime complexity, making it harder to resolve where political authority . . . resides" (Alter and Meunier 2009, 13). Keohane and Victor note that "regime complexes are marked by connections between the specific and relatively narrow regimes, but the absence of an overall structure or hierarchy that structures the whole set" (Keohane and Victor 2011, 8). While most authors are careful to note that this is not equivalent to a hollowing out of the nation state, it is unclear how we are meant to think about how these sources of authority are arranged beyond repeated conflicts that occur as the result of rule overlap.

Other works in the regime complexity literature do acknowledge some variation in hierarchy, though generally with different language-often focusing on how RCs range from fragmented to integrated. Integration implies some degree of hierarchy, though this is generally not made explicit. For example, Keohane and Victor argue that the RC for climate change is in the middle of the spectrum: some rules conflict, while others are "mutually reinforcing" (Keohane and Victor 2011, 7). Others examine how the degree of fragmentation or integration affects the configuration of authority (Biermann et al. 2009;Zelli 2011;Zelli and van Asselt 2013) and how institutions cope with these conditions (Oberthur and Stokke 2011;Gehring and Faude 2014). These studies acknowledge that configurations of authority vary across RCs, though confusingly, they often begin from a background assumption of nonhierarchical relations.

This study builds on the few works that explicitly reject the assumption of nonhierarchical regimes. For example, Henning's study of the Euro crisis notes that institutional proliferation does not necessarily result in fragmentation and that RCs are not fragmented by definition (Henning 2017, 19). Thus, RCs vary along this dimension. In addition, Pratt and Henning specify two dimensions of variation in RCs: hierarchy and differentiation. Hierarchy is "determined by authority patterns among international institutions," which can be defined at the time institutions are created or emerge over time (Henning and Pratt 2020, 7). Differentiation is the extent to which institutions vary in the functions they perform (Henning and Pratt 2020, 11). Low levels of differentiation imply substitutability. I expand upon this work by creating new measures and extending the analysis to include nonstate actors. 4Despite the relatively limited consideration of hierarchy in the RC literature, discussions in the broader IR literature note that it is a critical concept for understanding world politics. Lake argues that there are several dimensions of hierarchy in IR and, importantly, that a focus on sovereignty obscures larger questions of power and authority. He notes that "not all interesting actors within the international system are fully sovereign" (Lake 2003, 324). As such, we should abandon the concept of absolute authority and expand our conception of hierarchy to include non-state actors, which he refers to as polities. Similarly, Mattern and Zarakol note that "hierarchy-centered approaches to IR promise to deliver what anarchy-centered approaches have not: a framework for theorizing and empirically analyzing world politics as a global system rather than just an international one" (Mattern and Zarakol 2016, 624). This study imports these theoretical insights into the analysis of RCs by offering an empirical approach for operationalizing them.

sections.paper_id: Green - 2022 - Hierarchy in Regime Complexes Understanding Authority in Antarctic Governance
paragraphs: Second, most studies of complexity focus on formal rules created through intergovernmental negotiation rather than other governance activities. Raustiala and Victor focus on the assortment of multilateral agreements that govern plant genetic resources (Raustiala and Victor 2004). Alter and Meunier describe international regime complexity as "the presence of nested, partially overlapping and parallel international regimes that are not hierarchically ordered" (Alter and Meunier 2009, 13). Oberthur and Stokke describe institutional complexes "in which two or more international institutions interact to co-govern issue areas in international relations . . . and form interlocking structures" (Oberthur and Stokke 2011, 1). And Hooghe and Marks' work on multilevel governance analyzes the reconfiguration of authority based on jurisdiction, task, and membership-all of which are established by formal rules (Hooghe and Marks 2003). Indeed, most scholarly work that deals with the increasing density of institutions emphasizes interactions among rules created by states; interactions among actors is a secondary concern. 5The focus on formal rules is problematic for two reasons. First, in contrast to domestic systems, it is unclear which institution has the final authority to adjudicate conflicts (Alter and Meunier 2006, 365). Although there are some mechanisms to establish a hierarchy among laws, they are limited and subject to political constraints. Thus, a focus on formal rules will bias analysts toward the view that RCs are nonhierarchical. Since informal practices also play a key role in governance, emphasis on rules is apt to overlook key activities (Stone 2011;Kleine 2013;Henning 2017).

paragraphs: Third, though most IR scholars acknowledge the diversity of actors in global governance, studies of RCs tend to focus on multilateral regimes (Axelrod 2011;Kim 2013;Gómez-Mera 2017;Morin, Pauwelyn, and Hollway 2017;Farrell and Newman 2018;Morin and Jinnah 2018). However, global governance also includes private actors (Green 2014), NGOs (Bush 2015;Stroup and Wong 2017), publicprivate partnerships (Andonova 2010), transgovernmental networks (Slaughter 2004;Newman 2008), and advocacy networks (Hadden 2015), among others. However, with few exceptions, this has not been imported into work on regime complexity (exceptions include Abbott 2012; Henning 2017).

There is some recent work that moves beyond comparing "like with like" to consider a variety of "global governors" (Avant, Finnemore, and Sell 2010). For example, work by Roger, Hale, and Andonova examines the relationship between transnational climate governance and domestic policies (Roger, Hale, and Andonova 2017). This is a study of apples and oranges-different sources of authority at different levels of governance. 6 Similarly, work by Abbott, Green, and Keohane (2016) considers the nature of interactions between international organizational and private transnational regulatory organizations. Studying multiple types of governors indicates why a focus on formal rules is insufficient.

paragraphs: Finally, relatively few studies offer replicable measures of complexity. More frequently, there are works that qualitatively describe complexity (see, e.g., the special issues by Alter and Meunier 2009;Orsini, Morin, and Young 2013). Most of these works involve two parts: the actors involved and the interactions among them, with a heavy emphasis on the former. Without a systematic, transparent, and replicable approach to describing RCs, which would allow for comparison across cases, there is little inferential work to be done. In short, to date, rigorous empirical work is limited (for exceptions, see Widerberg 2016;Pratt 2018).

sections.paper_id: Green - 2022 - Hierarchy in Regime Complexes Understanding Authority in Antarctic Governance
paragraphs: In this section, I explain how hierarchy is systemic, emergent, and positional and provide observable implications for the framework. Hierarchy can be understood as "the extent of the authority exercised by the ruler over the ruled" (Lake 2009, 9). It occurs through the granting and ceding of authority, in which the ruled exchanges some freedom for political order provided by the ruler (Lake 2009, 29). In this view, hierarchy is a dyadic relationship between two actors, generally states. As Lake notes, a single state can "possess relationships of varying degrees of hierarchy across many dyads," yet the dyadic relationship is still the foundation of the concept (Lake 2009, 61). This approach, associated with liberal IR theory (Mattern and Zarakol 2016, 628), works well when all actors share the same fundamental attribute of sovereignty. However, such an approach becomes more difficult when one no longer compares "like with like"-as when many different types of actors are involved.

For this reason, I treat hierarchy as a systemic property, to distinguish it from traditional state-centered approaches that emphasize dyadic relations (for a similar approach, see Pouliot 2017). Waltz's theory of international politics is perhaps the most prominent example of IR's long tradition of conceptualizing politics through the lens of systems (Waltz 1979; see also Haas 1964;Jervis 1998). He defines a system as "a set of interacting units" (Waltz 1979, 43). Similarly, the RC literature studies interactions among institutions governing a specific issue area. In this case, hierarchy is systemic in that it is established through the interactions among organizations. I contrast it with dyadic views of hierarchy, as encapsulated by Lake's theory.

Hierarchy is also an emergent property. Like other complex systems, RCs are dynamic and can be viewed as "spontaneously evolving institutions" (Gehring and Faude 2013, 121). Hierarchy, therefore, emerges through a process of self-organization; thus, "order may prevail without an orderer" (Waltz 1979, 77). In the contractual view, "actors trade degrees of freedom for a desired social or political arrangement" (Mattern and Zarakol 2016, 634). They take individual decisions to cede authority to others (or not), which produce a set of authority relations that are not explicitly agreed upon ex ante. Hierarchy, therefore, emerges as a result of these decisions.

In a more expansive view, hierarchy is viewed as "deep structures of organized inequality" (Zarakol 2017, 7) that are not designed but rather occur as the result of "social practices, inter-subjective structures or a superstructure rooted in material inequality" (Zarakol 2017, 7). These practices and shared understandings may, or may not, proceed from explicit consent of the actors involved. In this conceptualization, hierarchy does not require consent to produce deference (Pouliot 2017, 131). By some accounts, this definition is too broad, since it transforms hierarchy into a residual category: "whatever is not egalitarian" (Donnelly 2017, 252). It is, therefore, critical to define how relations are unequal, which I address in the third section.

Hierarchy is also positional. Social network analysis demonstrates that hierarchy arises through variation in connections to others (Hafner-Burton, Kahler, and Montgomery 2009;Victor, Montgomery, and Lubell 2017). These connections, or ties, as they are referred to in SNA, can be social, economic, or material relationships or flows. For instance, shared membership in an organization is one way to conceive of a tie as is the volume of trade between two nations. Social network analysis is increasingly used as a tool to study IR (Hafner-Burton, Kahler, and Montgomery 2009;Kahler 2009;Maoz 2010;Green 2013;Hadden 2015;Greenhill and Lupu 2017;Kim 2020). For example, Slaughter notes that the "new world of international relations" is no longer one of grand strategy, symbolized by the chessboard, but rather one of networks (Slaughter 2017, 21).

In Lake's model, variation in autonomy is an indication of hierarchy between states. He notes, "the greater the number of policy areas that are legitimately controlled by the political authority, the more hierarchical the relationship" (Lake 2009, 52). As hierarchy increases, the autonomy of the subordinate actor decreases, and the subordinate is increasingly reliant on the dominant state for resources. Thus, "powerful states are powerful by virtue of the fact that they have attractive outside options" (Stone 2011, 21). The same holds true for other actors. These options, in turn, depend on their access to information, resources, and other actors (see, e.g., Keck and Sikkink 1998;Hadden 2015). The same principle applies in RCs, though multiple types of actors are present. In general, more ties yield access to more information and resources and, therefore, greater autonomy.

I suggest two different dimensions of hierarchy: deference among actors and variation in the autonomy of actors. Following Pratt, I maintain that RCs exhibit patterns of deference, which can be understood as acceptance of another actor's exercise of authority or jurisdiction (Gehring and Faude 2014;Efrat and Newman 2016;Pratt 2018). Often, deference occurs when one actor changes its behavior to accommodate another, due to overlapping activities or functions (Gehring and Faude 2014, 478). However, deference need not be explicitly agreed upon. For example, deference can occur before potential or actual overlap occurs, such as when organizations seek niches unoccupied by others in order to avoid conflict (Abbott, Green, and Keohane 2016). In both instances, deference results in self-organizationan emergent property of the system. This broader conceptualization is particularly useful when considering how deference works across different types of actors (Green 2014). In practice, this conceptualization of deference looks similar to Henning and Pratt's notion of differentiation-the extent to which different institutions are (non) substitutable. The third section demonstrates that deference results in a clear "sorting" of organizations by function-differentiating them by their respective activities and capabilities.

Hierarchy can also be understood as variation in autonomy. Though somewhat counterintuitive, autonomous actors are very well connected in an RC. They have access to a broad range of people, information, and other resources (Granovetter 1973;Stone 2011;Hadden 2015). They can serve as brokers or gatekeepers, providing or withholding access as they see fit (Burt 1995). By contrast, poorly connected actors are dependent on a few others for these same resources and thus are less autonomous. The greater the variation in autonomy across actors, the more hierarchy is present in the regime complex.

It is possible that, in practice, connections to many actors could serve as a constraint on those with high levels of autonomy. This could occur if the broker is linked to actors with diverse or competing preferences (Stovel and Shaw 2012). However, structurally speaking, they remain more autonomous than those with fewer connections. A number of network-based studies in international relations demonstrate the power and influence that stems from high levels of connectivity (see, e.g., Keck and Sikkink 1998;Maoz 2012;Murdie and Polizzi 2017). Moreover, theories of preferential attachment (neatly summarized by the phrase "the rich get richer") suggest that brokers have the possibility to circumvent any constraints they may encounter, while those on the periphery do not.

Although social science scholars distinguish between networks and hierarchies (Powell 1990; Barnett, Pevehouse and Raustiala 2021), SNA frequently points to hierarchical networks (Ravasz and Barabási 2003). These can be "scale free" (Barabasi and Bonabeau 2003), where a few nodes are much better connected than others. The result is a highly skewed distribution of connections among nodes, following a power-law distribution. Certain nodes are hubs with greater autonomy (Barbarási 2002). Moreover, hierarchical networks are modular: there are highly connected groups of nodes that have relatively few links outside the group. Thus, hierarchical networks have both hubs (specific actors with lots of ties) and clusters (smaller subgroups that are more highly connected to each other than to others outside the cluster).

Modularity and scale-freeness allow for both deference and autonomy. Hubs are well connected-consistent with having autonomy. However, they depend on less wellconnected actors, which is evidence of deference (Ravasz and Barabási 2003; figure 1). Hierarchy can also manifest as a "core and periphery" structure to the network, where a small group of actors are more closely connected, while others are more distant in the periphery. In this section, I explain how hierarchy is systemic, emergent, and positional and provide observable implications for the framework. Hierarchy can be understood as "the extent of the authority exercised by the ruler over the ruled" (Lake 2009, 9). It occurs through the granting and ceding of authority, in which the ruled exchanges some freedom for political order provided by the ruler (Lake 2009, 29). In this view, hierarchy is a dyadic relationship between two actors, generally states. As Lake notes, a single state can "possess relationships of varying degrees of hierarchy across many dyads," yet the dyadic relationship is still the foundation of the concept (Lake 2009, 61). This approach, associated with liberal IR theory (Mattern and Zarakol 2016, 628), works well when all actors share the same fundamental attribute of sovereignty. However, such an approach becomes more difficult when one no longer compares "like with like"-as when many different types of actors are involved.

For this reason, I treat hierarchy as a systemic property, to distinguish it from traditional state-centered approaches that emphasize dyadic relations (for a similar approach, see Pouliot 2017). Waltz's theory of international politics is perhaps the most prominent example of IR's long tradition of conceptualizing politics through the lens of systems (Waltz 1979; see also Haas 1964;Jervis 1998). He defines a system as "a set of interacting units" (Waltz 1979, 43). Similarly, the RC literature studies interactions among institutions governing a specific issue area. In this case, hierarchy is systemic in that it is established through the interactions among organizations. I contrast it with dyadic views of hierarchy, as encapsulated by Lake's theory.

Hierarchy is also an emergent property. Like other complex systems, RCs are dynamic and can be viewed as "spontaneously evolving institutions" (Gehring and Faude 2013, 121). Hierarchy, therefore, emerges through a process of self-organization; thus, "order may prevail without an orderer" (Waltz 1979, 77). In the contractual view, "actors trade degrees of freedom for a desired social or political arrangement" (Mattern and Zarakol 2016, 634). They take individual decisions to cede authority to others (or not), which produce a set of authority relations that are not explicitly agreed upon ex ante. Hierarchy, therefore, emerges as a result of these decisions.

In a more expansive view, hierarchy is viewed as "deep structures of organized inequality" (Zarakol 2017, 7) that are not designed but rather occur as the result of "social practices, inter-subjective structures or a superstructure rooted in material inequality" (Zarakol 2017, 7). These practices and shared understandings may, or may not, proceed from explicit consent of the actors involved. In this conceptualization, hierarchy does not require consent to produce deference (Pouliot 2017, 131). By some accounts, this definition is too broad, since it transforms hierarchy into a residual category: "whatever is not egalitarian" (Donnelly 2017, 252). It is, therefore, critical to define how relations are unequal, which I address in the third section.

Hierarchy is also positional. Social network analysis demonstrates that hierarchy arises through variation in connections to others (Hafner-Burton, Kahler, and Montgomery 2009;Victor, Montgomery, and Lubell 2017). These connections, or ties, as they are referred to in SNA, can be social, economic, or material relationships or flows. For instance, shared membership in an organization is one way to conceive of a tie as is the volume of trade between two nations. Social network analysis is increasingly used as a tool to study IR (Hafner-Burton, Kahler, and Montgomery 2009;Kahler 2009;Maoz 2010;Green 2013;Hadden 2015;Greenhill and Lupu 2017;Kim 2020). For example, Slaughter notes that the "new world of international relations" is no longer one of grand strategy, symbolized by the chessboard, but rather one of networks (Slaughter 2017, 21).

In Lake's model, variation in autonomy is an indication of hierarchy between states. He notes, "the greater the number of policy areas that are legitimately controlled by the political authority, the more hierarchical the relationship" (Lake 2009, 52). As hierarchy increases, the autonomy of the subordinate actor decreases, and the subordinate is increasingly reliant on the dominant state for resources. Thus, "powerful states are powerful by virtue of the fact that they have attractive outside options" (Stone 2011, 21). The same holds true for other actors. These options, in turn, depend on their access to information, resources, and other actors (see, e.g., Keck and Sikkink 1998;Hadden 2015). The same principle applies in RCs, though multiple types of actors are present. In general, more ties yield access to more information and resources and, therefore, greater autonomy.

I suggest two different dimensions of hierarchy: deference among actors and variation in the autonomy of actors. Following Pratt, I maintain that RCs exhibit patterns of deference, which can be understood as acceptance of another actor's exercise of authority or jurisdiction (Gehring and Faude 2014;Efrat and Newman 2016;Pratt 2018). Often, deference occurs when one actor changes its behavior to accommodate another, due to overlapping activities or functions (Gehring and Faude 2014, 478). However, deference need not be explicitly agreed upon. For example, deference can occur before potential or actual overlap occurs, such as when organizations seek niches unoccupied by others in order to avoid conflict (Abbott, Green, and Keohane 2016). In both instances, deference results in self-organizationan emergent property of the system. This broader conceptualization is particularly useful when considering how deference works across different types of actors (Green 2014). In practice, this conceptualization of deference looks similar to Henning and Pratt's notion of differentiation-the extent to which different institutions are (non) substitutable. The third section demonstrates that deference results in a clear "sorting" of organizations by function-differentiating them by their respective activities and capabilities.

Hierarchy can also be understood as variation in autonomy. Though somewhat counterintuitive, autonomous actors are very well connected in an RC. They have access to a broad range of people, information, and other resources (Granovetter 1973;Stone 2011;Hadden 2015). They can serve as brokers or gatekeepers, providing or withholding access as they see fit (Burt 1995). By contrast, poorly connected actors are dependent on a few others for these same resources and thus are less autonomous. The greater the variation in autonomy across actors, the more hierarchy is present in the regime complex.

It is possible that, in practice, connections to many actors could serve as a constraint on those with high levels of autonomy. This could occur if the broker is linked to actors with diverse or competing preferences (Stovel and Shaw 2012). However, structurally speaking, they remain more autonomous than those with fewer connections. A number of network-based studies in international relations demonstrate the power and influence that stems from high levels of connectivity (see, e.g., Keck and Sikkink 1998;Maoz 2012;Murdie and Polizzi 2017). Moreover, theories of preferential attachment (neatly summarized by the phrase "the rich get richer") suggest that brokers have the possibility to circumvent any constraints they may encounter, while those on the periphery do not.

Although social science scholars distinguish between networks and hierarchies (Powell 1990; Barnett, Pevehouse and Raustiala 2021), SNA frequently points to hierarchical networks (Ravasz and Barabási 2003). These can be "scale free" (Barabasi and Bonabeau 2003), where a few nodes are much better connected than others. The result is a highly skewed distribution of connections among nodes, following a power-law distribution. Certain nodes are hubs with greater autonomy (Barbarási 2002). Moreover, hierarchical networks are modular: there are highly connected groups of nodes that have relatively few links outside the group. Thus, hierarchical networks have both hubs (specific actors with lots of ties) and clusters (smaller subgroups that are more highly connected to each other than to others outside the cluster).

Modularity and scale-freeness allow for both deference and autonomy. Hubs are well connected-consistent with having autonomy. However, they depend on less wellconnected actors, which is evidence of deference (Ravasz and Barabási 2003; figure 1). Hierarchy can also manifest as a "core and periphery" structure to the network, where a small group of actors are more closely connected, while others are more distant in the periphery. Those in the periphery are lower in the hierarchy; they are more dependent on their connections, since there are comparatively few of them.

At the actor level, those with autonomy have a high level of "betweenness," which measures how many actors are connected to each other by a given actor (Borgatti, Everett, and Johnson 2013). These "connecting" actors serve as brokers and control the flow of resources. Montgomery reinterprets Lake's theory with a social network "twist" demonstrating how actor betweenness is evidence of autonomy (Montgomery 2016). Hierarchy is present "when the dominant state acts as a broker between the subordinate state and other states" (Montgomery 2016, 34). Without connections to these key actors, "subordinates" are left to fend for themselves. When a small number of actors have high levels of autonomy, we can consider the whole network hierarchical, since it means greater variation across actors within the network. Thus, though autonomy applies to individual actors, its variation contributes to hierarchy across the network.

This study uses betweenness centrality as a measure of autonomy, but other network attributes might be more applicable to different issues. For example, in a trade-related RC, degree centrality might be more important, since the number of trading partners potentially provides more leverage in negotiating terms. Closeness centrality, where an actor has a lot of direct (or short) connections to others, might be more appropriate for global health or security, since rapid responses may be required.

sections.paper_id: Green - 2022 - Hierarchy in Regime Complexes Understanding Authority in Antarctic Governance
Green - 2022 - Hierarchy in Regime Complexes Understanding Authority in Antarctic Governance
paragraphs: Antarctic governance is understudied in IR (for exceptions, see Peterson 1980;Young 1989). This is surprising given the fact that it has played a "catalytic and innovative role" in international environmental law, contributing to widely accepted practices surrounding scientific advisory processes, environmental impact assessments, inspection procedures, and environmental liability (Sands and Peel 2012, 712).

The Antarctic RC includes five multilateral agreements. The most important is the 1959 Antarctic Treaty (AT), in which signatories agreed that the Antarctic would be used exclusively for peaceful purposes and would be governed by the principle of the common heritage of mankind. Most significantly, states with territorial claims in the Antarctic agree to set them aside in the name of joint management. The AT also emphasizes the importance of scientific research, allowing all scientists and observers free access to all areas of the Antarctic, including other nations' installations.

Two related treaties regulate seals (1972) and fishing (1980). The fishing treaty was created under the authority of the United Nations (UN) Convention on the Law of the Sea. In addition, in 1991, parties created a protocol to the Antarctic Treaty, focused on environmental protection, following a failed effort to regulate mineral extraction.

Non-state actors involved in rulemaking include the International Association of Antarctica Tour Operators (IAATO), which creates guidelines for visitors and tour operators governing everything from wildlife observation to decontamination protocols. The Scientific Committee on Antarctic Research (SCAR) advises the AT and has developed codes of conduct to govern scientific research in Antarctica.

Governance activities are not limited to rulemaking. The Antarctic and Southern Ocean Coalition (ASOC) is an umbrella organization representing over thirty NGOs working on Antarctic-related issues. It conducts policy research, engages in advocacy, and participates in partnerships. IAATO also engages in other governance activities: it conducts technical trainings for vessels operating in the region, drafts and disseminates policy papers, and engages with other multilateral processes, including the International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships and International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea. Despite previous successes, there are a number of emerging challenges. First, the pace of regulation has slowed. The annual meeting of the Consultative Parties agrees to legally binding "Measures" to implement the AT, but these have narrowed in scope over time. Since 2004, almost all Measures are for management plans for protected areas and historic sites. 7 Second, the evolving issues of tourism and bioprospecting have virtually no formal rules. Bioprospecting has none, and the last Measure to address tourism was in 2009. Yet, Antarctic tourism has skyrocketed in the last decade; there are now more than 50,000 tourists each season (IAATO 2018). This poses new environmental threats: disruption of species and habitats, generation of pollution and waste, and introduction of invasive species. IAATO's voluntary guidelines address these issues, yet there are concerns that such codes are inadequate to the task at hand (Verbitsky 2013).

Third, companies and governments have begun actively prospecting for genetic material in the waters surrounding the Antarctic (Verbitsky 2013). Currently, there are no international regulations governing this activity, either in Antarctica or the high seas more generally. A new international treaty to govern bioprospecting in the high seas is currently being negotiated under the auspices of the UN Convention on the Law of the Sea, but prospecting activities in Antarctica continue apace.

Fourth, some rules have proven insufficient to protect vulnerable species. Krill are a keystone species for the Antarctic and a major food source for whales. However a rising use of krill for human consumption and aquaculture have led to a steady increase in catches over the last decade. 8Overall, the rise of commercial activities in Antarctica seems poised to overtake the emphasis on scientific inquiry. Understanding variation in authority provides insights into how these threats are currently being governed and whether this might produce robust regulation.

sections.paper_id: Green - 2022 - Hierarchy in Regime Complexes Understanding Authority in Antarctic Governance
paragraphs: Organizations are the unit of analysis in this study. I construct the universe of cases using the Yearbook of International Organizations, including only those that correspond to keywords Antarctic and Antarctica. 9 This yielded twenty-four organizations. I discarded those organizations that were not classified as international or regional, or that did not have any web presence after 2000, 10 resulting in a total of organizations to eleven (see table 1). Of the four multilateral agreements in force, only three are listed, since the Environment Protocol is legally an extension of the AT.

sections.paper_id: Green - 2022 - Hierarchy in Regime Complexes Understanding Authority in Antarctic Governance
paragraphs: I evaluate hierarchy both qualitatively and quantitatively. Qualitatively, I sent a brief email survey of the organizations about which ones they recognize as authoritative and with whom they work closely.foot_9 While the quantitative data provide the big picture of organizational interactions, survey responses offer more detailed information about how people within each organization perceive the position and authority of other organizations in the regime complex.

The email survey was sent to the general information email of the organization; if contact information for the Executive Secretary or Communications Officer was available, then additional emails were sent to them. The survey asked three open-ended questions: which organizations were the most authoritative, why they were considered to be authoritative, and which organizations they work with closely.

The intergovernmental organizations unsurprisingly affirm that they are the sole authoritative actors in the governance arena. The Assistant Executive Secretary of the AT describes the governing body of the treaty as "the only authoritative organization in Antarctic policy making and governance" (AT, email survey, May 7, 2019). Other respondents also affirm its importance. For example, SCAR notes that the AT is authoritative "because of its mandate," that is, the authority that states have delegated to it (SCAR, email survey, July 31, 2019). ASOC notes that the two intergovernmental organizations, the AT and the Convention on the Conservation of Antarctic Marine Living Resources (CCAMLR), "are the most authoritative in terms of final outcomes" (ASOC, email survey, May 23, 2019). The IAATO stated that it "uses the tools and framework set by the ATCM [Antarctic Treaty Consultative Meeting] and CEP [Committee on Environmental Protection] to develop/strengthen their own policies, even if ATCM Measures are not yet in force" (IAATO, email survey, November 24, 2020).

However, other organizations consistently identified SCAR and ASOC as key actors. One respondent referred to SCAR as the "authoritative voice" of the scientific community (ARC, email survey, May 14, 2019). Another noted that "its conclusions [sic] represent a broad scientific consensus" and that "there are few Consultative Parties who will contradict or dispute SCAR information" (ASOC, email survey, May 23, 2019). SCAR itself reported that it works with a broad range of organizations, including "Antarctic treaty bodies, scientific bodies e.g., World Meteorological Organisation, World Climate Research Programme . . . IAATO . . . ASOC" (SCAR, email survey, July 31, 2019). As demonstrated below, all of these findings are consistent with the quantitative analysis.

The email surveys also indicate that two organizations were inadvertently excluded through the sampling strategy. ASOC and CCAMLR work with the Coalition of Legal Toothfish Operators (COLTO), which was not included in the analysis. This industry organization promotes sustainable fishing practices of Patagonian toothfish, which has been overfished in the past, particularly through illegal practices. ASOC also mentioned its work with another industry group, the Association of Responsible Krill operators, but this organization was not discussed by anyone else. The email surveys are, therefore, useful for confirming not only the validity of the ties identified through the topic modeling but also the construction of the sample itself.

I now describe the topics that comprise the Antarctic RC and then show how actors are linked through shared topics. The patterns of connection among actors, via shared topics, demonstrate that the regime complex is systemic, emergent, and positional. Consistent with other IR systemic approaches, it examines a set of actors (the organizations) and the interactions among them (their work on shared topics). It is emergent because a division of labor emerges among organizations. It is positional in that there is variation in autonomy among them, as evidenced through three measures of network position: a strong core-periphery structure, variation in actor betweenness, and a skewed distribution of ties, suggesting a tendency toward scale freeness.

To reiterate, the shared topic itself is not an authority relation. Rather, it is the variation in ties among organizations through shared work on a topic that creates variation in autonomy, a key dimension of hierarchy. This study follows earlier IR work, which demonstrates how interactions among actors can circulate resources and in turn confer authority. Kahler notes, "the ties or links among the actors (nodes) create a structure . . . that in turn serves to constrain actors or provide opportunities for action" (Kahler 2009, 5). These differential constraints and opportunities then create variation in autonomy. Similarly, Hadden demonstrates that links to other organizations through shared events provide a mechanism to share information and exercise social influence (Hadden 2015). Hafner-Burton and Montgomery argue that preferential trade agreements create ties between states, and the distribution of those ties imbues some states with greater social power than others (Hafner-Burton and Montgomery 2008). Stroup and Wong show that "leading" NGOs possess access to information and ability to mobilize resources that lesser NGOs do not (Stroup and Wong 2017).

sections.paper_id: Green - 2022 - Hierarchy in Regime Complexes Understanding Authority in Antarctic Governance
paragraphs: The first step in measuring hierarchy is detecting the topics that comprise the RC; these provide the basis for the network analysis. Topic modeling shows precisely why one cannot look exclusively at rules to discover the issues that constitute Antarctic governance. While there are a variety of topics on issues regulated by intergovernmental agreements, there are also topics beyond those agreements. For example, there are no provisions in any of the Antarctic treaties about marine search and rescue, climate change, or tourism. Yet, they appear as topics in the model. This provides evidence for the earlier point that comparing "like with like" means that we will likely miss important governance activities-even in a relatively circumscribed issue area such as the Antarctic.

The topics fall into four thematic areas: scientific exploration, species and habitat protection, tourism, and intergovernmental cooperation. Table 2 inductively groups the topics by the thematic area, omitting seven topics that do not fit into these categories. Thus, there are three "tiers" of governance activity: the individual topics (e.g., boat tourism or ATCM rulemaking), the thematic areas (designated by the grouping of topics), and the RC as a whole (all of the topics combined). The existence of topics demonstrates the "parts" within the system but does not tell us anything about the interactions among them. To understand the nature of interactions, we must examine ties between actors and topics.

sections.paper_id: Green - 2022 - Hierarchy in Regime Complexes Understanding Authority in Antarctic Governance
paragraphs: Hierarchy is a mode of organization that encourages deference (Lake 2009, 11). In a fragmented governance system, actors perform similar functions, and there can be overlap and conflict (Biermann et al. 2009). In an RC, actors may sort themselves into different thematic areas to reduce overlap, consistent with the notion that hierarchy is emergent. This process may be the result of explicit deference but need not be. The result is a division of labor, which may occur be-cause of adjustment after overlap or niche-seeking beforehand. I expect that research organizations (as designated in table 1) will work primarily in the scientific research and species and habitat thematic areas. There is no a priori prediction for the umbrella NGO organization. If there were no deference to others, we would expect that each organization is equally likely to work on a given topic. While this is not particularly surprising, it demonstrates how the SEP framework lends much more specificity to the functioning of the RC beyond the notion of overlap.

Figure 1 shows the distribution of thematic areas across organizations, representing one way of understanding the emergent pattern of organization in Antarctic governance. 13 It is evident that organizations do not simply work on all issues but rather that they self-organize according to mandates, capabilities, and preferences.

The left-hand cluster of organizations represents research organizations. As expected, they work predominantly on topics related to scientific research: data collection, temperature monitoring, and geological surveying. The middle cluster is Secretariats of multilateral agreements. The largest thematic area for all three is intergovernmental cooperation. Even in the case of the AT, where thematic areas are more evenly distributed, intergovernmental cooperation is a larger proportion of the documents (17.7 percent) than species and habitat protection (16.3 percent). Finally, the "other" category shows, unsurprisingly, that the tourism organization works primarily on tourism issues. ASOC, the NGO umbrella organization, is spread relatively evenly across the thematic areas.

sections.paper_id: Green - 2022 - Hierarchy in Regime Complexes Understanding Authority in Antarctic Governance
paragraphs: In the rest of the analysis, I use SNA to detect hierarchy. Data collection on organizational covariates allows an analysis of which organizations are working on which topics. I estimate the expected proportion of documents on each topic by organization with a 90 percent confidence interval. The higher the proportion, the more work the organization likely does on the topic. When the confidence interval includes zero, we can conclude that the organization does not work on that topic.

This information is then transformed into an affiliation matrix that shows which actors are "affiliated with" (i.e., produced documents related to) a given topic. Proportions are dichotomized. A "1" indicates a statistically significant proportion of the document generated by the organization on a given topic. 14 Measuring the proportion of topics in a document is important, since it does not simply privilege those organizations with more documents.

Thus, the analysis is conducted on twice-permuted data. The organizational covariates demonstrate which organizations work on which topics. The affiliation matrix then shows which topics are shared by a given set of actors. A tie represents shared activity on a given topic and the possibility for interaction; two organizations that work on the same topic are thus linked together. Table 3 provides an illustration of the data transformation. The columns represent organizations active in the RC; the rows are the topics (excerpted) as generated by the topic model. A "1" indicates that the organization works on a given topic. Ties in and of themselves are not an indication of authority; rather, recall that it is the variation in ties across organizations that generates authority relations. This is why hierarchy is systemic rather than dyadic. Network ties create opportu-nities and constraints; in the aggregate, these can be understood as sources of authority.

The relationships are depicted graphically in the affiliation network in figure 2. In the upper panel, the larger circles represent organizations and the smaller ones are topics. The ties indicate which organizations work on which topics (i.e., have a statistically significant proportion of documents related to a given topic). The color-coding demarcates different communities. Communities have dense connections with other actors within the community and sparser connections with those outside. This is consistent with the notion that both deference and autonomy can coexist in a network. Ties that differ in color from the organization from which they originate indicate a link to a different community, i.e., a position of brokerage. Panel (b) shows the strength of the ties between organizations, where thicker lines mean more shared topics. Larger nodes have higher betweenness centrality. 15 Three findings stand out. First, the node size of the organization varies with its "hub" score. Higher hub scores indicate connections to more "high-quality" or authoritative nodes. Clearly, SCAR, ATS, and ASOC have the most ties to other communities. They have access to a variety of types of actors through their shared work and, thus, greater levels of autonomy than other organizations in the RC. Second, there are five distinct communities-organizations and topics with greater density than the overall network. This is consistent with a division of labor. Different organizations are more likely to work on some issues than others. Indeed, there is a community of non-state actors, dominated by SCAR and ASOC, a community of intergovernmental actors (dominated by ATS and CCAMLR), and two for different scientific organizations. (IAATO is an industry group, but also works on science-based tourism guidelines.) Third, panel (b) demonstrates how a subset of actors are more closely connected, as illustrated by the thickness of the ties that connect them. The different colors also connote different communities, where ties are denser than the average density of the overall network. The most autonomous nodes are the largest ones: ASOC, ATS, SCAR, and, to a lesser extent, CCAMLR. They are all densely connected by numerous shared topics; IAATO, Convention for the Conservation of Antarctic Seals (CCAS), and Council of Managers of National Antarctic Programs (COMNAP) are also part of this community, though clearly with much less autonomy than the other four organizations.

Variation in autonomy is further confirmed by the coreperiphery matrix (figure 3). Dark cells indicate that the organization works on the topic; white cells indicate no work on the topic. The density rates in each block reinforce what figures 2 and 3 demonstrate graphically. The core block of topics and actors-the top left corner of figure 3has a density of 91 percent, meaning that 91 percent of all possible ties between actors and topics are present. In the most peripheral block, the lower right, the equivalent figure is 13 percent. Actors in the core are much more connected than those in the periphery. In a "perfect" core-periphery model, the core (upper-left quadrant) should have a density of one and the periphery (lower-right quadrant) a density of zero. The densities reported here indicate that this is a well-fitted model.

A third way to assess autonomy is to see which actors have the highest "betweenness centrality." An actor with high betweenness centrality falls on the shortest pathway between other pairs of actors. These actors occupy a brokerage position: they can facilitate or end the transmission of information or other resources between actors.

Flow betweenness is a more expansive approach, which accounts for all possible paths through a given actor, not just the shortest ones. It assesses all available pathways between two actors when a third actor blocks them. Table 4 shows the standardized flow betweenness for all actors. Overall, the network centralization is low, suggesting that there is no single dominant actor (a star network is maximally centralized, with a centralization score of one). However, the flow betweenness scores of ASOC and ATS are almost two standard deviations above the mean, indicating that these two actors have much more autonomy than the rest. They serve as important brokers to the other organizations in the RC. This indicates that the relatively low centralization is a function of the large amount of variation in autonomy across actors-more evidence of hierarchy.

A final indication of autonomy is illustrated by the extent to which the distribution of the ties is skewed disproportionately to a small number of actors. This is a "scale-free" network, where certain nodes are better connected than others. The formal test for a scale-free network requires showing that the power law characterizes the distribution of ties (Ravasz and Barabási 2003). Due to the small size of the network, this test is not possible. Instead, I map the degree distribution in the affiliation network, which indicates the qualities of a scale-free network. Degree denotes the number of ties per node, that is, how many topics a given organization works on. A scale-free network has a few high-degree nodes; when presented graphically, there should be a right-skewed distribution. Figure 4 demonstrates that the distribution is consistent with a scale-free network.

sections.paper_id: Green - 2022 - Hierarchy in Regime Complexes Understanding Authority in Antarctic Governance
sections.header_raw: What Hierarchy Means for Governing Antarctica
sections.header_norm: what hierarchy means for governing antarctica
paragraphs: Clearly, the regime complex for Antarctica is not simply a mass of interconnected actors and institutions. However, what exactly do these patterns of hierarchy tell us about the ability of Antarctic regime complex to address the emerging issues described in the second section? I suggest four key conclusions.

First, the fact that bioprospecting does not emerge as an issue in the topic modeling is worrisome. The deep seabed is governed by the principle of common heritage of mankind, which states that certain resources belong to all people. Yet, bioprospecting in seamounts and seafloors surrounding in Antarctica has been occurring for two decades with little in the way of regulation. Unchecked, this continued activity could pose a threat to the RC's legitimacy and the deep-sea environment.

Second, the considerable deference to IAATO-the tourism industry group-is also cause for concern. Figure 1 shows not only that IAATO works most on the issue of tourism, but also that other organizations have very little activity on this topic. Despite being the main regulatory body, only 8 percent of ATS documents address tourism. This is not because tourism is a minor issue-as the rapid growth in tourists demonstrates. Rather, it is consistent with the high level of deference to IAATO on the topic. They have an outsized impact on the issue, which, given their interests, seems likely to give rise to regulatory capture or soft rules, which give them considerable leeway in their activities (Mattli and Woods 2009).

Third, the division of labor shows that the majority of organizations are working on issues related to scientific research or habitat and species protection-further evidence of the neglect of growing commercialization issues. Given that scientific cooperation was one of the main pillars of the original treaty, it is not surprising to see this borne out in the division of labor. However, there is a real regulatory gap emerging.

Finally, there is considerable hierarchy present in the Antarctic regime complex, and this seems promising for addressing the regulatory gap. The ATS, along with SCAR and ASOC, are clearly the key actors in Antarctic governance; should they decide to shift their activities to address these new issues, they have the real potential for influencing governance activities.

paragraphs: Though important for environmental and security reasons, the governance of Antarctica is admittedly not a central topic of world politics. What are the implications of this analysis for studying other areas of world politics? First, one might argue that the case of Antarctica is an outlier, since it has a dominant intergovernmental organization-the AT. However, the Antarctic case is relevant to many areas of world politics where a diversity of "global governors" are a large part of the institutional landscape, such as climate change, finance, and internet governance (Avant, Finnemore, and Sell 2010). The observed hierarchy in this case not simply the product of traditional state-based authority. The other two most authoritative organizations are non-state actors.

Second, the SEP framework provides insights into what is being governed-applicable to any area of world politics. The Antarctic case shows that there are issues being governed that are not part of the system of formal rules and, thus, clear regulatory gaps. As a number of studies have demonstrated, regulatory gaps provide opportunities for new actors to exercise or expand their influence (Avant, Finnemore, and Sell 2010).

Third, the proliferation of actors in Antarctic governance, as in other areas of global governance, may provide unanticipated pathways for governance. For example, Slaughter describes how transnational networks of regulators have organized to facilitate global cooperation on banking, finance, and security, among other areas (Cashore, Auld, and Newsom 2004;Green 2014;Abbott, Green, and Keohane 2016). In particular, they have been able to harmonize rules across jurisdictions, effectively creating deference to a centralized (and sometimes semiformal) organization.

Inclusion of a diversity of actors may also reveal how some of them may exercise authority "under the radar." For instance, the Investor State Dispute Settlement (ISDS) mechanism is a form of public international law but has recently been transformed into an area of governance where private actors exercise considerable authority, through the growing role of third-party funders backing firms' international legal claims against countries (Hale 2015). As Kalyanpur and Newman note, this shift "demonstrates the way in which financialization has extended well beyond traditional economic sectors, reconfiguring the practice of international law" (Kalyanpur and Newman 2021, 2). This example suggests an emergent hierarchy that empowers a new set of actors-firms. The SEP framework can be applied to detect similar processes in other issue areas.

sections.paper_id: Green - 2022 - Hierarchy in Regime Complexes Understanding Authority in Antarctic Governance
paragraphs: Scholars see complexity in all corners, but if all governance is complex, then complexity simply becomes a background condition of world politics. In this paper, I offer an empirical strategy for systematically measuring complex governance, as a first step toward cross-issue comparison. This is an important contribution in and of itself, since most work to date treats complexity either as a dichotomous variable or as an underspecified continuum. Providing a replicable method for mapping the regime complex for Antarctica, moves the study of complexity toward "observable facts" rather than the analyst's perceptions (Gehring and Faude 2013, 120).

Empirical rigor is important, but only if it is in the service of important larger questions. This paper engages with three key questions about global governance: What is being governed? Who has authority? How is authority organized? These are descriptive questions, which allow us to specify the dependent variable with greater precision and variation.

Although we tend to privilege causal inference, the importance of good description should not be underestimated (King et al. 1994, 34-35). By understanding how authority is organized in increasingly dense webs of organizations and actors, we can begin to formulate theories about the causes and effects of the observed variation. Many scholars of world politics are interested in whether and how regime complexity matters for the purposes of addressing global problems and the protection of global commons; the concepts and tools presented in this article help advance that research agenda.

Moving forward, the SEP framework can potentially be applied to other issue areas, though there are empirical challenges. Assembling the universe of relevant organizations requires delimiting boundaries, which in turn depends on linkages between issues areas, changing problem definitions, and the existence and effectiveness of boundary organizations (Keohane and Victor 2011;Muzaka 2011;Morin et al. 2017). However, given the proliferation of studies on various RCs, scholars could begin from the existing compilations of organizations, use surveys to generate a population of organizations, or scrape the web for linkages between organizations (Carpenter 2011; Carpenter and Jose 2012). Ties may also vary by issue area, but a number of SNA-based studies provide useful models, examining shared membership, trade flows, and joint attendance at events (Hafner-Burton and Montgomery 2006;Bernstein et al. 2010;Hadden 2015). While the boundary challenge may make cross-issue comparison difficult, if identical processes are used, such a comparison should be feasible.

In sum, this analysis is a first step in a much larger set of questions about understanding how hierarchy functions in RCs and in global governance more broadly. By understanding how different types of actors interact to produce patterns of hierarchy, we can better see how order is established. Importantly, this order can occur through practice as well as through the design of international institutions.
